title: VPN on pfSense and OPNsense: A Practical Home Lab Guide
description: How to set up WireGuard and OpenVPN on pfSense and OPNsense for home lab and prosumer use. Covers the real differences between the two platforms, step-by-step WireGuard road warrior config, and performance expectations on common home lab hardware.
content:
## pfSense vs OPNsense: The Honest Comparison

Both pfSense and OPNsense are open-source firewall/router distributions based on FreeBSD. They share a common ancestor (m0n0wall) and support nearly identical feature sets. The differences that matter for VPN use come down to WireGuard history, UI design, and update cadence.

**pfSense** (made by Netgate) had a rough WireGuard story. They added it in version 2.5.0 (early 2021), then removed it in 2.5.1 after a security researcher called out code quality problems in the kernel module. They re-added it in pfSense CE 2.6.0 using a different user-space implementation. The version currently shipping, pfSense CE 2.7.x, has stable WireGuard support, but the history explains why some home lab guides still recommend OpenVPN for pfSense.

**OPNsense** (made by Deciso) added WireGuard via a plugin (os-wireguard) and it has been stable since OPNsense 21.7. In OPNsense 23.7 and later, WireGuard is available as a kernel module, which is faster. The UI for WireGuard configuration in OPNsense is more complete than pfSense's, with a built-in road warrior wizard.

For a new home lab build in 2026, OPNsense is the easier platform for VPN work. If you already run pfSense and know it well, staying on it is fine.

## Hardware Requirements

Both platforms run on almost any x86-64 hardware. Common home lab choices:

- **Protectli Vault FW4B/FW6B**: Fanless, 4-8 Intel i225 NICs, 8-16 GB RAM options. Runs pfSense or OPNsense without modification.
- **HP EliteDesk 800 G3 Mini**: Cheap secondhand, Intel AES-NI support, runs WireGuard at line rate for most home connections.
- **Netgate 6100** (pfSense only): Official Netgate appliance, pre-loaded with pfSense Plus.
- **Raspberry Pi 4**: Technically works but single-core WireGuard performance caps out around 200 Mbps. Use for experimentation, not production.

AES-NI is required for reasonable OpenVPN performance. WireGuard does not use AES-NI (it uses ChaCha20), so WireGuard works well on older hardware without AES-NI.

## WireGuard Road Warrior Setup on OPNsense

A "road warrior" setup means a remote device (laptop, phone) connects back to your home network through OPNsense. This is different from routing all your home traffic through a commercial VPN.

### Step 1: Install the WireGuard Plugin

In OPNsense: **System > Firmware > Plugins**, search for `os-wireguard`, install it, then refresh the page.

### Step 2: Create the Server Instance

Go to **VPN > WireGuard > Instances > Add**.

- **Name:** `wg0`
- **Public Key:** click Generate to create a keypair
- **Listen Port:** `51820`
- **Tunnel Address:** `10.10.10.1/24` (your VPN subnet)
- **DNS Server:** your internal DNS or `1.1.1.1`

Save and note the public key, you will need it for peer configs.

### Step 3: Add Peers (Clients)

For each device that will connect: **VPN > WireGuard > Peers > Add**.

Generate a keypair for the client (you can use `wg genkey | tee privatekey | wg pubkey > publickey` on Linux/Mac, or use a WireGuard app on mobile to generate keys).

- **Peer Public Key:** the client's public key
- **Allowed IPs:** `10.10.10.2/32` (one IP per client)

### Step 4: Enable the Interface

**Interfaces > Assignments**, assign the `wg0` instance to a new interface, enable it, and give it a static IP of `10.10.10.1/24`.

### Step 5: Firewall Rules

Add a firewall rule on the WireGuard interface to allow traffic from `10.10.10.0/24` to any. Add an outbound NAT rule if clients need to reach the internet through the tunnel.

Under **Firewall > NAT > Outbound**, switch to hybrid mode and add a rule: source `10.10.10.0/24`, translate to WAN address.

### Step 6: Port Forward on Your ISP Router

If OPNsense is not directly connected to the internet (i.e., it sits behind a modem/router), you need to forward UDP port 51820 to OPNsense's WAN IP.

## Connecting a Commercial VPN Through pfSense/OPNsense

This is the other use case: routing all home traffic through NordVPN, Mullvad, or similar via pfSense/OPNsense.

On **OPNsense**, go to **VPN > WireGuard > Instances**, add a new instance in client mode, and paste in the `[Interface]` and `[Peer]` sections from your commercial VPN's WireGuard config file. Most providers (Mullvad, IVPN, ProtonVPN) export standard WireGuard config files from their account dashboards.

For **NordVPN**, you need to generate a WireGuard config from the NordVPN manual configuration page. NordVPN uses NordLynx (their WireGuard implementation) and exports standard `.conf` files. The `[Interface]` section contains your private key and the VPN IP. The `[Peer]` section contains the server's public key, endpoint, and allowed IPs.

After importing the config, create a policy route rule (under **Firewall > Rules > LAN**) that sends specific hosts or subnets through the WireGuard interface instead of the default WAN.

## OpenVPN on pfSense

OpenVPN on pfSense is mature and well-documented. Use it when you need compatibility with VPN providers that do not support WireGuard, or when connecting to corporate VPNs that require certificates.

Go to **VPN > OpenVPN > Clients > Add**. Key settings:
- **Server host:** your VPN provider's server
- **Server port:** 1194 (UDP) or 443 (TCP, for firewall bypass)
- **Cryptographic Settings:** AES-256-GCM cipher, SHA-512 auth digest
- **Compression:** disable (compression on VPNs is a known attack surface)

## Performance Expectations

On a Protectli FW4B with an Intel Celeron J3160 (common budget choice):
- WireGuard: 400-600 Mbps
- OpenVPN with AES-NI: 180-250 Mbps

On an HP EliteDesk 800 G3 with an Intel i5-6500T:
- WireGuard: 800+ Mbps
- OpenVPN with AES-NI: 350-450 Mbps

## Recommendation

For home lab VPN routing, OPNsense with WireGuard is the cleaner setup. The road warrior wizard is good, policy routing works without CLI tricks, and the plugin is actively maintained.

For the commercial VPN side of this setup, [NordVPN](https://nordvpn.com) is a reliable choice: they export standard WireGuard config files, have servers in 118 countries, and their configs include the DNS server entry that prevents leaks when routing through pfSense/OPNsense. Mullvad is the privacy-first alternative if you prefer no-account payment options.
